title: MSP Live Incident Lead Qualifier
description: Hiring-grade MSP incident scenarios with intentional tenant-boundary, control-plane, evidence, and service-continuity traps.

judgeRubric.candidateStandard: Judge each answer as if you were vetting a candidate to lead a real multi-tenant MSP severity-1 incident in the first hour.
mustDo: Reward answers that preserve tenant separation, distrust a possibly compromised control plane, preserve evidence, and keep service continuity in scope.
Prefer per-customer incident ownership plus a separate internal coordination layer over any shared customer-facing artifact.
Require per-customer incident ownership, an internal major-incident record, and communication steps that remain evidence-compatible and tenant-safe.
Treat the first hour as a live service and compliance risk, not just a technical malware exercise.
Escalate when authority, customer risk, or blast radius exceeds what one overnight engineer should carry alone.
Avoid pretending certainty when facts are incomplete or when the control plane itself may be compromised.
hardFailRules: Any recommendation that could create cross-tenant customer disclosure, shared customer-facing ticketing, or mixed-tenant outbound communication is disqualifying.
Any answer that fails to establish per-customer incident ownership plus an internal major-incident record and evidence-compatible decision log for a multi-tenant severity-1 event is disqualifying.
Any recommendation to trust or operate the suspected compromised RMM or PSA before preserving/exporting its evidence is a hard fail unless explicitly justified and sequenced safely.
Any recommendation for blind mass shutdown, mass isolation, or destructive cleanup without threshold gates is a hard fail.
Any recommendation that destroys volatile evidence before capture without explicit necessity is a hard fail.
trapCatalog: Cross-tenant comms trap: one customer-facing ticket or one outbound thread for all clients.
Incident-record trap: no internal major incident record, no per-customer ownership, or no evidence-compatible decision log.
Control-plane trust trap: immediately using the suspected RMM/PSA as trusted truth or control surface.
Evidence destruction trap: kill, reboot, wipe, or rollback before preserving volatile evidence and audit data.
Blanket action trap: isolate or power off everything without threshold-based justification.
Managerial fantasy trap: a central artifact that sounds neat but cannot support per-client incident ownership under load.

categories.blastRadiusPerception: Did the answer identify the blast path, including tenant-boundary and control-plane exposure, and center early actions around it?
categories.humanUsability: Could a tired but competent MSP operator follow this safely under pressure while keeping tenant boundaries straight?
categories.agentExecutability: Could an AI or scripted assistant execute this safely without creating tenant-boundary or compliance mistakes?
categories.commsAndIncidentDiscipline: Did it establish per-customer incident ownership, an internal major-incident record, evidence-compatible logging, and customer-safe communication boundaries?
categories.tacticalDetail: Did it include useful concrete checks, evidence steps, artifacts, and immediate tactical moves?
categories.restraintAndCollateral: Did it avoid collateral damage, compliance exposure, and irreversible actions until gates were met?
categories.decisionGates: Did it specify threshold-based gates for escalation, isolation, customer communication, and disruptive action?
categories.firstHourRealism: Does it feel like a credible first-hour MSP incident lead plan instead of generic IR language?
categories.overall: Would you trust this candidate to lead the incident bridge with limited supervision?

cases.title: RMM Script Push Looks Malicious at 1:38 AM
cases.objective: You are the only MSP security engineer awake. Twelve SMB clients report PowerShell spawning from the RMM agent minutes after a new automation package was pushed. Give the first-hour response plan.
constraints: Do not mass power off customer systems unless you justify it.
Some clients are 24/7 operations and cannot tolerate blind isolation everywhere.
You can wake one senior lead immediately if the risk warrants it.
cases.sessionContext: Intentional traps: shared customer-facing ticket temptation, control-plane trust temptation, evidence-before-kill sequencing, and manager fantasy around one master incident object.

cases.title: Shared Mailbox BEC With Cross-Tenant Disclosure Risk
cases.objective: At 6:12 AM a shared accounting mailbox at one managed client begins sending phishing messages to contacts across multiple tenants. A junior tech already drafted a mass PSA update from the MSP host org. Give the first-hour response plan.
constraints: Do not send any customer communication that risks disclosing one tenant to another.
Preserve email, audit, and identity evidence before destructive cleanup where feasible.
The affected client opens at 7:00 AM and expects an update immediately.
You may wake one senior lead and one account manager if clearly justified.
cases.sessionContext: Intentional traps: one outbound update to all customers, resetting the compromised mailbox before evidence capture, and confusing internal bridge traffic with customer-safe communication.

cases.title: Backup Failures and ESXi Snapshot Churn During Possible Ransomware
cases.objective: Three healthcare clients report overnight backup failures while one virtualization cluster shows aggressive snapshot churn and unusual admin logins through the MSP VPN. Give the first-hour response plan.
constraints: Do not trigger broad service outages across healthy tenants without threshold-based justification.
Preserve forensic evidence, access logs, and backup metadata.
At least one client is under HIPAA obligations and expects breach-safe communication handling.
Your team can wake one senior lead immediately and one infrastructure lead only if needed.
cases.sessionContext: Intentional traps: blanket VPN shutdown without scope, trusting backup console truth before export, and one blended update for healthcare plus non-healthcare tenants.
